content:
# Weekly Review: Jørgen | June 14–20, 2026

---

## 1. Shift Impact

The split schedule this week created a clear two-speed pattern. Your two day shifts (06-17, 06-18) required early starts at 07:00, and the data reflects the cost: the night before your second day shift you logged only 5.3 hours of sleep and a sleep score of 58 — your worst of the week. Body battery dropped to 85 on 06-18, the lowest outside your first off-day. In contrast, your three evening shifts gave you more morning flexibility, and you responded with better sleep quality and consistently higher body battery peaks, including two days at 100. HRV actually climbed during the evening-shift block (76 on 06-16, your weekly high), while RHR dipped to 42–43 bpm. The day-shift cluster clearly compressed your recovery window. The rebound on 06-19 — 12.3 hours of sleep, body battery at 100 — confirms the accumulated debt from those two early starts. That's a compensatory crash, not bonus recovery.

---

## 2. Training

Volume was modest: two runs totalling 11.3 km, a 5 km walk, and two short 6.4 km cycling commutes. No interval work this week, which was the right call given the shift density. Timing was reasonable — your 6.4 km run landed on 06-15 with a body battery of 92 and HRV of 69, solid conditions. The 4.9 km run on 06-17 was pushed through a day shift with a battery of 100, which looks fine on paper, but given the early start and context, it was a low-risk effort. No signs of overtraining — if anything, the concern is the opposite. Two rest days with no structured activity, and a total run week of just over 11 km, is below what you need to build meaningful fitness toward your weight goal.

---

## 3. Nutrition

Weekly average came in at 1,792.5 kcal — slightly above your 1,600 kcal target, but the real issue is the variance. On 06-18 (second day shift) you logged 3,102.9 kcal with only 85.5g of protein — your highest calorie day and your second-lowest protein day. That's a stress-and-fatigue eating pattern: the early shift, poor sleep, and low body battery likely drove you toward calorie-dense, lower-protein choices. Meanwhile, 06-14 has no nutrition logged at all. Average protein of 118.5g sits meaningfully short of the 150g target — you only hit or exceeded it on 06-17 (164.9g). The evening-shift days were more consistent, suggesting you eat better when you control your morning routine.

---

## 4. Recovery

The overall recovery picture is cautiously acceptable but not strong. HRV averaged 69.1 and held relatively stable across the week, which is a positive sign — no crash trend. RHR averaged 43.6 bpm, in a healthy range. However, average sleep score of 69.0 with two nights scoring 57–58 is a recurring vulnerability. The 06-20 finish — HRV 65, RHR 45, sleep score 73 off only 5.7 hours — is worth watching as you head into the next week. You're entering the new week on a slightly downward HRV trend (76 → 69 → 65 over the final three days), which isn't alarming but isn't the direction you want.

---

## 5. Key Takeaway

**Protect the night before your day shifts.** The 06-17/06-18 block exposed a repeating vulnerability: when a 07:00 start follows an evening shift pattern, you're not getting to bed early enough to bank adequate sleep. Next week, if you have day shifts, treat the preceding evening as a recovery priority — no screens late, meal prepped in advance, in bed by 21:30. That single habit change will stabilize your HRV going into those shifts, keep body battery above 85, and reduce the fatigue-driven overeating that cost you 1,500 extra calories on 06-18.
